title: In Front
description: Whispering Cave, ch.3. The chivalric skeleton knight in the antechamber. Fighter's solo duel — the fight the ch.3 prose has been narrating in past-tense until now. Filename _ch2 is vestigial; location is the cave.

steps.text: The chamber opened wide enough that the torchlight didn't reach the far wall. In the middle of the floor, on a chalk-line that must have been drawn once and re-drawn many times, a skeleton in old plate armor was waiting. It was, when it saw them, standing up.

The skeleton did not attack. The skeleton bowed. It was not the bow of something that had forgotten what a bow was. It was the bow of something that had done the same bow, in the same room, to a great many parties, over a great many years.

fighter.text: The lead was already unbuckling the shield strap for a tighter fit. They had understood what the bow was before the bow was finished.

lines.speaker: Bard
lines.text: It's a *duel.* Look at the chalk line.

default.text: The Fighter stepped past the chalk line without ceremony, drew his sword, and returned the bow. His bow was less practiced. He had not been doing this for years. He had, however, been preparing to do this for years — which was, the skeleton seemed to accept, close enough.

lines.speaker: Fighter
lines.text: The rest of you — back. This one is mine.

default.text: He didn't argue for it. He didn't explain why. The skeleton had asked for a duel and the Fighter was the one who understood what a duel meant. Somebody had to be. Somebody usually was.

steps.text: The skeleton fell the way it had bowed — deliberately, without flourish, in a manner that suggested the falling had also been rehearsed. Its plate armor rang against the stone once, and then was quiet.
The Fighter stood over it for a moment longer than the moment required. Then he sheathed the sword, turned toward the party, and did not, for a beat, quite meet anyone's eyes.

Later, when the others were examining the chalk line and the Bard was writing down the shape of the skeleton's bow, the Fighter looked at his own hands. They were shaking. He watched them for the space of a breath. Then he set them on the pommel of the sword and they were not shaking. He had not decided to do that. It was simply what the hands did when they were told.
The Fighter was, the party would learn, the kind of person who counted what mattered and trusted the rest.
